event: Homo erectus, known as Java Man, inhabits the island of Java, marking one of the earliest hominin presences in the region.
Anatomically modern humans settle the Indonesian archipelago, leaving cave art in Sulawesi among the oldest figurative paintings on Earth.
Austronesian peoples migrate into the archipelago from Taiwan and the Philippines, bringing agriculture, pottery, and seafaring traditions.
Bronze and iron Dong Son culture spreads through the islands, evidenced by ceremonial bronze drums and metalworking.
The Hindu kingdom of Salakanagara, one of the earliest organized states in western Java, emerges according to tradition.
The Tarumanagara kingdom flourishes in western Java, leaving Sanskrit stone inscriptions attesting to early Indianized statehood.
Chinese pilgrim Yijing records the maritime Buddhist empire of Srivijaya centered at Palembang in southern Sumatra.
King Sanjaya founds the Mataram Kingdom in central Java, establishing a Hindu dynasty.
Construction begins on Borobudur, the colossal Buddhist temple in central Java built under the Sailendra dynasty.
The Hindu temple complex of Prambanan is built in central Java, dedicated to the Trimurti.
King Airlangga reunifies and rules the eastern Javanese kingdom of Kahuripan after a period of fragmentation.
Ken Arok founds the Singhasari kingdom in eastern Java after overthrowing Kediri.
Raden Wijaya founds the Majapahit Empire after repelling a Mongol invasion sent by Kublai Khan.
Hayam Wuruk ascends the Majapahit throne, presiding with Gajah Mada over the empire's greatest expansion across the archipelago.
Chinese admiral Zheng He visits the archipelago, strengthening trade and Muslim communities in coastal Java.
Portuguese forces capture Malacca, opening European competition for the lucrative spice trade.
Forces under Fatahillah seize the port of Sunda Kelapa and rename it Jayakarta, the future Jakarta.
The Majapahit Empire collapses as Islamic sultanates rise, completing the transition to a predominantly Muslim Java.
The Dutch East India Company (VOC) is chartered, beginning sustained European commercial dominance in the spice trade.
Jan Pieterszoon Coen seizes Jayakarta and founds Batavia as the VOC's colonial capital.
The Dutch capture Malacca from Portugal, consolidating their grip on archipelago trade routes.
The Treaty of Giyanti splits the Mataram Sultanate into the courts of Yogyakarta and Surakarta under Dutch influence.
The bankrupt VOC is dissolved and its territories are nationalized, beginning direct Dutch state colonial rule.
British forces under Stamford Raffles seize Java during the Napoleonic Wars, instituting brief British rule.
Prince Diponegoro launches the Java War, a major anti-colonial uprising against the Dutch lasting five years.
The Dutch impose the Cultivation System, forcing Javanese peasants to grow cash crops for export.
The volcano Krakatoa erupts catastrophically, killing tens of thousands and altering global climate.
Budi Utomo is founded, marking the beginning of the organized Indonesian nationalist awakening.
The Youth Pledge proclaims one nation, one people, and one language, Bahasa Indonesia, galvanizing the independence movement.
Imperial Japan invades and occupies the Dutch East Indies during World War II, ending Dutch colonial control.
Sukarno and Mohammad Hatta proclaim Indonesian independence on 17 August, days after Japan's surrender.
The Netherlands formally recognizes Indonesian sovereignty after a four-year armed and diplomatic revolution.
Indonesia joins the United Nations and adopts a unitary republican constitution.
Indonesia hosts the Bandung Conference of Asian and African nations, helping launch the Non-Aligned Movement.
Sukarno introduces Guided Democracy, concentrating power in the presidency.
Western New Guinea is transferred from Dutch to Indonesian control, later formalized as the province of Papua.
An attempted coup triggers mass anti-communist killings in which hundreds of thousands are murdered.
General Suharto assumes power and Indonesia co-founds ASEAN to promote regional cooperation.
Indonesia invades East Timor following Portugal's withdrawal, beginning a long and contested occupation.
Suharto's New Order regime formally annexes East Timor as Indonesia's 27th province amid international dispute.
The Asian financial crisis devastates the Indonesian economy, sparking widespread unrest.
Mass protests and riots force Suharto to resign after 31 years, beginning the Reformasi democratic era.
East Timor votes for independence in a UN-supervised referendum, ending Indonesian occupation.
Bombings in Bali kill over 200 people, the deadliest terrorist attack in Indonesian history.
A massive Indian Ocean earthquake and tsunami devastate Aceh, killing more than 160,000 Indonesians.
Susilo Bambang Yudhoyono wins Indonesia's first direct presidential election.
A peace agreement ends the decades-long Aceh separatist insurgency, granting the province special autonomy.
Joko Widodo is elected president, the first Indonesian leader from outside the political and military elite.
Indonesia hosts the G20 summit in Bali and passes a law to relocate the national capital to Nusantara in Kalimantan.
